paper_title: Liberating LLM Capabilities in Full-Duplex Speech Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Speech-based large language models are typically constrained to spoken replies, which limits their user-facing outputs to what can be verbalized and suppresses text-native capabilities such as code generation, structured analysis, and multi-step reasoning in realtime interaction, for tasks that require persistent, structured, and inspectable intermediate outputs. Existing work improves spoken reasoning or full-duplex turn-taking, but still treats text as a hidden intermediate state or a subordinate modality rather than a first-class output channel. We propose Listen-Write-Speak (LWS), a text-first tri-channel paradigm in which a single autoregressive LLM continuously listens to user audio, writes visible free-form text as its primary output, and speaks a realtime oral response in parallel under a shared causal attention context. This behavior is implemented entirely through a Token Schema, requiring no architectural modifications, and learned via a two-stage data pipeline that synthesizes per-second cognitive annotations consistent with the revealed input timeline. Empirically, LWS demonstrates strong full-duplex interaction on Full-Duplex-Bench, reaches 4.72 on VoiceBench AlpacaEval, achieves 92.6% writing-speaking consistency, and consistently outperforms its internal ablations on URO-Bench. These results suggest that visible writing can serve as a first-class output channel for speech interaction without sacrificing realtime responsiveness. The code and dataset are available on the project page: https://royalzhang.com/project/lws-page/.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 4 ] . [ 1 7 4 5 7 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r Liberating LLM Capabilities in FullDuplex Speech Models Luoyuan Zhang, Bokai Xu, Junbo Cui, Weiyue Sun, Yingjing Xu, Hanyu Liu, Yuan Yao contact: zly.idleness@gmail.com Project Page

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Speech-based large language models are typically constrained to spoken replies, which limits their user-facing outputs to what can be verbalized and suppresses text-native capabilities such as code generation, structured analysis, and multi-step reasoning in realtime interaction, for tasks that require persistent, structured, and inspectable intermediate outputs. Existing work improves spoken reasoning or fullduplex turn-taking, but still treats text as hidden intermediate state or subordinate modality rather than first-class output channel. We propose Listen-Write-Speak (LWS), text-first tri-channel paradigm in which single autoregressive LLM continuously listens to user audio, writes visible free-form text as its primary output, and speaks realtime oral response in parallel under shared causal attention context. This behavior is implemented entirely through Token Schema, requiring no architectural modifications, and learned via two-stage data pipeline that synthesizes per-second cognitive annotations consistent with the revealed input timeline. Empirically, LWS demonstrates strong full-duplex interaction on Full-Duplex-Bench, reaches 4.72 on VoiceBench AlpacaEval, achieves 92.6% writingspeaking consistency, and consistently outperforms its internal ablations on URO-Bench. These results suggest that visible writing can serve as first-class output channel for speech interaction without sacrificing realtime responsiveness. The code and dataset are available on the project page: https://royalzhang.com/project/lws-page/.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Human communication is inherently multimodal, yet speech and writing do not share the burden equally. Decades of work in cognitive science, CSCW, and multimodal HCI suggest that different representational media support different functions: speech is especially effective for turn-taking, grounding, and pragmatic coordination, whereas written or visual representations are better suited to precise, structured, persistent, and spatially organized information (Clark & Brennan, 1991; Oviatt, 1999; Larkin & Simon, 1987; Scaife & Rogers, 1996). In practice, the two modalities are almost always co-present: collaborative software engineering revolves around shared source files that participants discuss verbally; scientific presentations pair spoken narrative with projected figures and equations; and professional meetings produce written minutes that outlive the spoken exchange. The underlying principle is consistent: speech orchestrates the interaction, while anything that must be exact, revisitable, or spatially organized is written down and laid out. Large language models are, fundamentally, text-native systems that share this affinity for writing. Many of their most distinctive capabilities, including code generation, structured analysis, mathematical derivation, and professional writing, are defined and most naturally expressed within the unconstrained space of written text. However, when these models interact with users through speech, fundamental bottleneck arises: their outputs must be projected into the much narrower subspace of speakable language. If developer says, write me binary search in Python, text-based LLM can return clean, executable code; *Corresponding authors. 1 Table 1: Comparison of speech-LLM paradigms. FD: full duplex interaction; FT: free text outputs; CL: cognition while listening; CS: cognition while speaking. Prior models cognition is hidden internal thinking; LWS exposes it as visible text output. Paradigm models FD FT CL CS Think-Before-Speak Interleaved Think-Speak Dual-Brain Think-Speak Think-While-Listen Full-Duplex Parallel Text-Speech Multi-Channel Protocol Step-Audio 2, TVS STITCH, Mini-Omni-Reasoner MPS SHANKS, Chron. Think. Moshi, LSLM, FlexDuo Qwen3-Omni, Kimi-Audio OpenAI Harmony Listen-Write-Speak Ours Chronological Thinking only; others are half-duplex. speech-based LLM, by contrast, can only dictate that code aloud, forcing the user to transcribe each token manually. Likewise, when meeting participant asks, summarize the key decisions, the ideal response may be structured Markdown table, yet speech interface must linearize it into spoken list that is difficult to revise. In both scenarios, the speech modality suppresses precisely those forms of output that make LLMs uniquely useful the very forms that human collaborators would never entrust to speech alone. growing body of work has sought to mitigate this limitation by introducing reasoning into speech-based LLMs in several ways: before speaking (Wu et al., 2025a; Woo et al., 2025), interleaved with speaking (Chiang et al., 2025b; Xie et al., 2025), during listening (Chiang et al., 2025a; Wu et al., 2025b), or through full-duplex designs (Defossez et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2025). Despite their diversity, these approaches share common premise: text remains in the role of supporting speech. In other words, reasoning is used as an internal or auxiliary process to improve the spoken response, rather than as writing: first-class, user-visible, editable output channel through which the model directly expresses its full capabilities. Our contributions are fourfold: We formulate Listen-Write-Speak (LWS), text-first tri-channel interaction paradigm in which single autoregressive LLM continuously listens to user audio, writes visible text as the primary output, and speaks realtime response in parallel under one shared causal context  (Table 1)  . We introduce Token Schema that realizes this tri-channel behavior entirely through special tokens in standard autoregressive Transformer, requiring no architectural changes. We develop two-stage data pipeline that synthesizes per-second annotations designed to remain causally consistent with the progressively revealed input timeline. We show that LWS demonstrates strong full-duplex interaction on Full-DuplexBench, reaches 4.72 on VoiceBench AlpacaEval, achieves 92.6% writingspeaking consistency, and consistently outperforms its internal ablations on URO-Bench.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: The works most related to ours fall into three categories: reasoning-augmented speech LLMs, full-duplex spoken interaction, and multi-channel output architectures. These directions address complementary aspects of the problem and are often evaluated under different criteria. Table 1 summarizes them along four capability dimensions relevant to our setting. 2 Figure 1: Listen-Write-Speak (LWS) tri-channel architecture. The interaction is partitioned into temporal Units, within which three channels operate concurrently under shared causal context: Listening, Visible Writing, and Speaking. The writing channel remains active across both phases, appearing as listening-side writing during user speech and as reply-side writing during response, while speech is emitted only in speaking Units. 2.1 Reasoning-augmented speech LLMs Recent speech LLMs incorporate explicit reasoning, but differ in when the reasoning is performed. Think-Before-Speak models such as Step-Audio 2, Step-Audio-R1, and TVS (Wu et al., 2025a; Tian et al., 2025; Woo et al., 2025) reason before producing speech; this can improve response quality, but it increases latency and provides no cognition during the users turn. Interleaved Think-Speak models such as STITCH and Mini-Omni-Reasoner (Chiang et al., 2025b; Xie et al., 2025), as well as dual-model designs such as MPS (Wu et al., 2025c), move part of the reasoning process into reply generation, but still couple cognition to the speaking phase rather than to persistent user-visible channel. Think-While-Listen models such as SHANKS and Chronological Thinking (Chiang et al., 2025a; Wu et al., 2025b; Shih et al., 2025) perform reasoning during user speech, but this reasoning is introduced to support subsequent speech generation rather than as persistent, user-visible, free-form reasoning process; accordingly, the explicit cognition ceases once reply generation begins. 2.2 Full-duplex speech interaction Another line of work studies full-duplex spoken interaction. Models such as Moshi, LSLM, and FlexDuo (Defossez et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2025; Liao et al., 2025) emphasize overlap handling, turn-taking, and low-latency response. They can listen while speaking, but typically expose only speech as the user-facing output modality. Cascaded ASRLLMTTS pipelines preserve strong text generation quality, but they usually revert to turn-based interaction and therefore do not provide genuine full-duplex behavior. 3 2.3 Multi-channel output architectures Recent multimodal architectures show that speech systems can emit multiple concurrent streams. Parallel text-speech models such as Qwen3-Omni, DrVoice, and Kimi-Audio (Xu et al., 2025; Tan et al., 2025; Ding et al., 2025) generate text alongside speech, and protocolbased interfaces such as OpenAI Harmony (OpenAI, 2025) expose additional structured channels. However, these channels are mainly used for synthesis, control, or hidden analysis, rather than as an always-on free-form writing channel visible to the user. As summarized in Table 1, prior systems generally sacrifice at least one of four capabilities relevant to our setting: full-duplex interaction, free-text output, cognition while listening, or cognition while speaking. Our formulation differs in treating visible writing as first-class channel that remains active across both listening and speaking.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: 3.1 Architecture overview The gap identified in Related Work suggests common limitation of prior speech LLMs: they typically preserve only part of the functionality required for rich real-time interaction, and many rely on additional architectural machinery that makes the resulting systems harder to scale and reuse. Our starting point is that these four target capabilities need not be implemented as four separate mechanisms. Instead, they can be organized as three concurrent channels that run within one full-duplex autoregressive process: Listening continuously processes the users incoming audio, Visible Writing externalizes the models text-native output as user-visible free-form text, and Speaking verbalizes realtime oral response. Under this view, an always-on Listening channel provides full-duplex interaction; an always-on Visible Writing channel provides unconstrained text output and can already serve as an externalized working memory while the user is still speaking; and the joint activation of Writing and Speaking during response generation yields cognition while speaking. LWS realizes this design in standard autoregressive Transformer without introducing extra decoders or cross-channel fusion modules. The system operates entirely at the text-token level: user speech is represented as discrete audio tokens, the model emits speech tokens for the Speaking channel, and their hidden states are passed to frozen TTS module that lies outside the training objective. This division keeps the learning problem centered on language modeling, rather than multimodal alignment or speech generation. As illustrated in Figure 1, the interaction timeline is partitioned into discrete intervals called Units. The Unit duration is configurable hyperparameter; we use 1 second in our experiments (5). Within each Unit, up to three channels operate concurrently: Listening (Perception) Channel: Continuously ingests the users audio stream, represented as fixed number of audio tokens per second. This channel is always active, enabling full-duplex interaction. Visible Writing (Cognition) Channel: Always active, externalizing the models textnative cognition as unconstrained free-form text. During listening it builds incremental reasoning that sharpens the subsequent response; during speaking it emits structured artifacts (code, tables, derivations) in parallel with natural speech. Speaking (Expression) Channel: Generates natural spoken language as real-time oral response of the visible-writing output. This channel is active only during speaking. Each Unit exists in one of two states: Listening Unit: Listening + Visible Writing (ls cogn). The model ingests audio and incrementally builds an understanding of the users intent. Speaking Unit: Listening + Speaking + Visible Writing (reply cogn). The model simultaneously listens, speaks, and produces user-visible free-form text. The three channels share single attention context: all tokens (audio, visible-writing text, and spoken text) participate in the same causal attention computation. This implicit shared 4 attention serves as the information fusion mechanism across channels, requiring no additional cross-channel alignment modules. 3.2 Token schema design The core design principle of LWS is to encode the tri-channel visible-writing architecture entirely through token-level structure, rather than architectural modifications. We define set of special tokens that delineate channel boundaries within each Unit: Listening Unit Speaking Unit <unit> (cid:124) audio (cid:122) (cid:125)(cid:124) (cid:123) a1 a10 <lc> writing (cid:122) (cid:125)(cid:124) (cid:123) t1 tk </lc> </unit> (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) Listening Unit audio (cid:122) (cid:125)(cid:124) (cid:123) a1 a10 <spk> <unit> (cid:124) speak (cid:122) (cid:125)(cid:124) (cid:123) s1 sm <eos> <rc> (cid:123)(cid:122) Speaking Unit writing (cid:122) (cid:125)(cid:124) (cid:123) r1 rn </rc> </unit> (cid:125) We use abbreviated tags in equations for readability: <lc> </lc> <spk> ls cogn bos ls cogn eos speak <rc> </rc> <eos> reply cogn bos reply cogn eos chunk eos (1) (2) How one Unit runs in practice During each interval, the model first receives the current audio chunk. If the interaction is still in the users speaking phase, the Unit is instantiated as Listening Unit: the model opens the ls cogn segment and emits incremental visible writing based only on the audio observed so far, such as partial understanding, disambiguation, or short notes. The user can therefore see text appear on screen even before the model begins speaking. Once the model enters the response phase, subsequent intervals become Speaking Units: the model continues ingesting the next audio chunk, emits spoken output in the speak segment, and produces richer visible writing in the reply cogn segment, such as code, tables, or structured analysis. Thus, across successive Units, LWS behaves as an always-listening system whose visible writing remains active throughout the interaction, while speech is activated only in those Units assigned to response generation. Why separate the listening and speaking phases natural alternative is to use single unified cognition tag throughout the interaction. We instead separate the two cognitive phases because they are conditioned on different information states and occupy different positions in the causal timeline: one phase reflects ongoing interpretation of the users incoming speech, while the other additionally conditions on the models own emerging response. Making this distinction explicit simplifies next-token prediction and helps reduce information leakage across phases in full-duplex interaction. formal information-theoretic analysis is provided in Appendix C. 3. Inference Inference proceeds as continuous loop of Units through an asynchronous pipeline. In each Unit, the incoming user audio chunk is encoded by streaming Whisper encoder (Radford et al., 2023) into audio embeddings, which are then fed to the LLM together with the structural delimiter <unit>. The first token generated by the LLM (<lc> or <spk>) determines whether the interval is realized as Listening Unit or Speaking Unit. In Listening Unit, the model emits ls cogn text based only on the audio observed so far. In Speaking Unit, the model first generates speak tokens; after the segment ends at <eos>, the corresponding hidden states are forwarded to an external TTS module, while the LLM continues generating reply cogn tokens in parallel. The Unit then closes and the next audio 5 chunk begins. This design reduces response latency in three ways: audio is processed incrementally rather than after full-turn completion, speech synthesis starts as soon as spoken segment is available, and visible writing can continue while TTS is already speaking. Because audio ingestion never stops during this process, user speech that arrives while the model is speaking is still encoded into subsequent Units, which gives the system built-in mechanism for interruption handling without relying on an external VAD. Consequently, the system sustains real-time interaction with minimal mode-switching overhead, while preserving continuity across listening, speaking, and visible-writing updates.

sections.title: 4 Data Construction
sections.content: Training LWS requires data with per-second cognitive annotations aligned to an audio timeline, format that does not exist in public corpora. We therefore design two-stage pipeline that starts from standard text QA pairs and converts them into the Unit-based interaction format used by the model. Stage 1 (Offline cognitive synthesis) begins from text conversation pair and uses strong teacher LLM to synthesize three text streams that can later be aligned to secondby-second interaction timeline. The first stream, streaming reasoning chain, represents incremental understanding during the users turn and is used to supervise ls cogn. The second, voice response, is concise spoken-style paraphrase used to supervise speak. The third is the original structured response itself, which is preserved as the supervision target for reply cogn. To make these streams consistent with streaming inference, we estimate plausible speech duration, segment the interaction into per-second steps, and prompt the teacher with strict causal constraint: at each second t, it may reason only from the portion of the input that would already have been heard by that time. Stage 2 (Online timeline construction) combines the Stage 1 text streams with real audio recordings and their CTC-based character-level alignments to construct the final Unit sequence. We first build global second-by-second timeline for each conversation, including random silence intervals between turns so that training examples reflect more natural conversational pacing. Each interval is then assigned as either Listening Unit or Speaking Unit according to who is currently speaking, and the corresponding text stream is inserted into the appropriate channel. To expose the model to overlap and take-over behavior during training, we additionally apply interruption augmentation to subset of conversations. The final dataset comprises 500K Chinese and English training examples in the 1-second Unit-based token format; the Stage 1 prompt template is provided in Appendix D.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Experimental setup Base model We build LWS on top of the MiniCPM-V architecture (Yao et al., 2024) with Qwen3-8B backbone LLM (Yang et al., 2025). The Listening channel uses streaming Whisper Medium encoder (Radford et al., 2023) as the audio perception module (APM), with audio tokens produced at rate of 10 tokens/second and pooled with stride of 5. For the Speaking channels TTS, we employ LLaMA-based TTS backbone with FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022), using S3Tokenizer (Du et al., 2024) as the audio tokenizer. The TTS module conditions on both the LLMs output tokens and projected hidden states. The APM and TTS module are frozen throughout the training process. The model is initialized from pre-trained checkpoint and fine-tuned for the tri-channel LWS capability. Training data We construct training data using the two-stage pipeline described in 4. The Stage 1 cognitive synthesis is applied to joint interleaved multimodal corpus covering both Chinese and English. After augmentation (including interruption augmentation), the final training set contains 500K examples. The data is formatted into the Unit-based token schema with 1-second Unit duration, interleaved audiotext chunks. 6 Table 2: URO-Bench results comparing representative speech LLM baselines and our three variants. U: understanding, R: reasoning, O: oral. Higher is better. Best score within each language block and column is in bold. Method Chinese GPT-4o-Audio GPT-Realtime Kimi-Audio Qwen-Omni Step-Audio 2 Listen-Write-Speak w/o write while listen w/o write while speak English GPT-4o-Audio GPT-Realtime Kimi-Audio Qwen-Omni Step-Audio 2 Listen-Write-Speak w/o write while listen w/o write while speak Basic Pro O Avg O Avg 89.4 88.8 79.3 59.7 91.1 79.3 65.9 73.4 90.2 87.4 83.4 66.3 92.7 83.3 76.6 74. 65.5 72.9 64.7 69.7 75.5 72.4 72.5 75.9 75.9 84.1 42.3 69.6 76.5 69.6 72.2 72.2 85.2 90.8 79.8 77.3 86.1 96.1 95.3 71. 90.4 94.1 60.4 76.2 84.9 92.7 92.4 66.3 78.6 80.6 73.6 69.0 83.3 82.6 77.9 73.7 84.5 88.1 60.0 70.6 83.9 81.9 80.4 71. 70.6 72.3 60.4 59.0 74.8 92.5 86.0 93.9 60.7 59.7 50.3 44.5 64.9 74.6 75.4 73.0 57.2 62.6 59.3 59.8 63.2 85.9 84.4 84. 64.4 74.5 40.6 63.9 67.8 89.0 84.6 81.3 70.2 74.2 76.2 58.7 65.1 75.5 74.6 63.6 78.5 76.1 56.0 49.4 66.3 70.3 66.8 52. 67.1 70.6 66.0 59.1 68.3 84.6 81.7 80.6 67.5 68.9 49.8 51.0 66.1 78.0 75.6 68.8 Training configuration We train on 32 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with bf16 mixed precision and gradient checkpointing enabled for both the full model and the LLM backbone. The LLM learning rate is 5 106 with cosine restart scheduler, minimum learning rate 1 106, and linear warmup from 1 108 over 200 steps. We use dynamic batching with maximum sequence length of 4,096 tokens and gradient accumulation over 2 steps. Only the LLM parameters are tuned; the APM and TTS module are frozen throughout training. Unit duration We set the Unit duration to 1 second (i.e., 1 fps) throughout all experiments. This choice balances writing output budget per Unit against real-time responsiveness. The architecture imposes no constraint on this value; shorter Units yield finer-grained interaction at the cost of less writing content per Unit, while longer Units allow deeper per-step reasoning but increase response granularity. 5.2 Main results 5.2.1 Spoken understanding and reasoning To evaluate spoken understanding and reasoning under multilingual and difficulty-stratified settings, we additionally report results on URO-Bench (Yan et al., 2025), whose Reasoning (R) dimension covers mathematical and logical reasoning tasks and thus directly measures structured reasoning ability. We compare LWS against representative public speech LLMs, and further include two internal ablations: w/o write while listen (disabling visible writing during listening, i.e. removing ls cogn) and w/o write while speak (disabling visible writing during speaking, i.e. removing reply cogn). The public baseline numbers are taken from the Step-Audio 2 technical report (Wu et al., 2025a). Table 2 shows mixed but informative pattern. Compared with public baselines, LWS is especially strong on harder Chinese splits, achieving the best Chinese Pro average (84.6), the best Chinese Pro understanding and reasoning scores (92.5/85.9), and the highest Chinese 7 Table 3: Comparison with existing speech LLMs on VoiceBench AlpacaEval Model GPT-4o-Audio VITA-1.5 Step-Audio Freeze-Omni GLM-4-Voice Listen-Write-Speak Score 4.78 4.21 4.13 4. 3.97 4.72 Table 4: Full-Duplex-Bench results across four interaction settings. TOR: take-over rate. FRQ: backchannel frequency. JSD: Jensen-Shannon divergence. Arrows indicate whether lower or higher is better. Model Public baselines PersonaPlex dGSLM Moshi Freeze-Omni Gemini Live 2.0 (deprecated) GPT-Realtime Listen-Write-Speak Pause Backchannel Turn Taking Interruption Syn.TOR Can.TOR TOR Freq JSD Can.TOR Lat. TOR Score Lat. 0.58 0.93 0.98 0.64 0.26 0.01 0.01 0.66 0.94 0.98 0.48 0. 0.12 0.01 0.33 0.69 1.00 0.64 0.09 0.00 0. 0.03 0.02 0.00 0.00 0.01 0.01 0.00 0.65 0.93 0.96 1.00 0. 0.98 1.00 0.99 0.98 0.94 0.34 0.66 1.00 0. 0.07 0.35 0.27 0.95 1.30 1.47 0.48 1.00 0.92 1.00 0.87 0. 0.97 0.99 4.21 0.20 0.77 3.62 3.38 3.85 4. 0.40 2.53 0.26 1.41 1.18 1.50 0.65 oral-basic score (96.1). On English Basic, GPT-Realtime remains strongest in average score (88.1), but LWS remains competitive. Within our model family, LWS outperforms both ablations on all four displayed Basic/Pro averages across Chinese and English (Chinese Basic: 82.6 vs. 77.9/73.7; Chinese Pro: 84.6 vs. 81.7/80.6; English Basic: 81.9 vs. 80.4/71.1; English Pro: 78.0 vs. 75.6/68.8), supporting the value of visible writing during both listening and speaking phases in this benchmark setting. 5.2.2 Reply quality We further assess reply quality using the official leaderboard on the AlpacaEval (Li et al., 2023) split of VoiceBench (Chen et al., 2024), reported in Table 3. VoiceBench follows speech-to-text evaluation protocol: the model receives spoken input, but the scored output is text. The resulting scores therefore reflect the quality of the Visible Writing (reply cogn) channel rather than spoken fluency alone. LWS achieves 4.72, outperforming all listed open-source baselines and trailing GPT-4o-Audio (4.78) by only 0.06. 5.2.3 Channel consistency potential risk of producing both speak and reply cogn from the same context is selfcontradiction: the spoken response may diverge from the visible written response. We evaluate this risk on AlpacaEval (Li et al., 2023) samples from VoiceBench (Chen et al., 2024) using an LLM-as-judge protocol, where GPT-5 is given the user query, the Visible Writing output (reply cogn), and the Speaking output (speak), and assigns 1 if the spoken answer is factually consistent with the written one and 0 otherwise. LWS achieves channel consistency rate of 92.6% (589/636), indicating that the two user-facing channels remain well aligned in the large majority of cases and that introducing visible writing does not materially compromise response coherence. 5.2.4 Full-duplex interaction Table 4 summarizes Full-Duplex-Bench results. In smooth turn-taking, Listen-Write-Speak reaches 0.97 Candor TOR at 0.48 latency, slightly behind the fastest specialized models such as PersonaPlex and Moshi, but substantially faster than larger commercial realtime models while maintaining competitive turn-taking quality. Under user interruption, it attains 4.02 GPT-4o quality at 0.65 latency, indicating that it can recover from overlap and continue with coherent responses without incurring the large delay observed in several baselines. On pause handling, it also matches the best reported TOR (0.01) on both the synthetic and Figure 2: Per-channel training loss curves. (a) ls cogn (listening-phase writing), (b) speak (speaking), (c) reply cogn (reply-phase writing), (d) overlay of all three channels. All channels converge smoothly, with no optimization instability in the joint setting. Candor settings. This pattern is consistent with the design goal of keeping ls cogn active during listening: the model can continuously form response plan before taking the turn, which helps preserve response quality when the interaction becomes dynamic. 5.2.5 Training convergence fundamental question is whether the tri-channel token schema can be stably trained within single autoregressive LLM. As shown in Figure 2, we decompose training loss by channel. All three channels converge smoothly with low variance (coefficient of variation < 8%). The converged losses are 1.16 (ls cogn), 0.93 (speak), and 0.84 (reply cogn), respectively. reply cogn converges fastest and to the lowest loss, consistent with its role as standard text generation conditioned on rich context. ls cogn starts highest but converges stably, reflecting the greater difficulty of incremental audio comprehension. The overlay in Figure 2(d) shows no obvious optimization instability in the joint setting, supporting the feasibility of trichannel joint training within single autoregressive LLM in our setup.

sections.title: 6 Limitations
sections.content: LWS combines full-duplex listening, visible writing, and real-time speaking within standard autoregressive framework, but the present study remains subject to several limitations. (1) Reasoning depth remains constrained by real-time operation. Because visible writing and speech must be produced within each Unit, the system is optimized for responsiveness and is not yet well suited to longer-horizon reasoning, multi-step planning, or complex tool-mediated workflows, where stronger performance may require slower response generation or an explicit mechanism for deferring speech while deeper written reasoning unfolds. (2) The input interface remains narrow. The current formulation assumes speech-only user input, limiting applicability in settings where users naturally combine speech with code, screenshots, tables, or other context. Extending LWS to adaptive multimodal input is therefore an important direction for future work.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented Listen-Write-Speak (LWS), text-first tri-channel paradigm that unifies fullduplex listening, visible writing, and real-time speaking within single autoregressive LLM. The design is realized entirely through token schema without architectural changes, supported by practical two-stage data pipeline. Empirically, LWS performs strongly on Full-Duplex-Bench, reaches 4.72 on VoiceBench AlpacaEval, achieves 92.6% writing speaking consistency, and outperforms its internal ablations on multilingual URO-Bench. These results support visible writing as first-class output channel for speech interaction in realtime settings while preserving standard autoregressive modeling interface in practice.

sections.title: Ethics Statement
sections.content: Listen-Write-Speak changes not only what speech model outputs, but also how users may trust it: because LWS can respond fluently in speech while simultaneously producing polished written artifacts (e.g., code, structured analysis, or derivations), it may increase the perceived authority and actionability of incorrect, biased, or unsafe content, especially in real-time settings where users may act on the spoken summary before fully inspecting the written output. The visible-writing channel improves inspectability relative to speech-only models, but it is not itself guarantee of correctness, faithfulness, or safety; accordingly, we recommend deployment with synchronized moderation across both spoken and written channels, policy-based refusal for hazardous requests, logging and auditing of model outputs, and clear user disclosure that visible writing is an assistive intermediate artifact rather than verified ground truth. Our training pipeline uses synthetic per-second annotations constructed from existing textaudio resources and does not rely on private user conversations, but any real-world deployment that processes live voice should still be governed by consent, retention, and access-control policies, since spoken interactions may contain sensitive personal information. We used external LLMs only as data-construction and evaluation tools: Qwen3-235B was used in Stage 1 to synthesize per-second cognitive annotations and spoken-style paraphrases under the causal prompt template in Appendix D, and GPT-5 was used as an LLM judge for the channel-consistency analysis in 5.2.3; the reply-quality evaluation prompts are listed in Appendix E. We also used Cursor as an AI-assisted development environment to help with manuscript polishing and code-related engineering work; it served only as productivity aid, and all resulting text and code were reviewed by the authors. These external tools are not components of LWS itself. We aim to release enough detail to support reproducibility while avoiding operational details that would materially lower the barrier to misuse.

Shanks: Simultaneous hearing and thinking for spoken language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.06917, 2025a. sections.title: A Inference Pipeline and Token Trace
sections.content: This appendix provides text-only walkthrough of the inference pipeline. In each Unit, the users audio is encoded by streaming Whisper encoder (Radford et al., 2023) into audio embeddings, which are fed to the LLM together with <unit> delimiter. The LLM then autoregressively generates the Units content: the first token it produces (<lc> or <spk>) autonomously determines the Unit type (Listening or Speaking). In Speaking Unit, speaking tokens are generated first; once the speaking segment ends at <eos>, its hidden states are immediately forwarded to an external TTS module running in separate thread, and the LLM continues generating visible-writing tokens (reply cogn) in parallel with TTS synthesis. The Unit closes with </unit> and the next Unit begins. In other words, the runtime consists of three asynchronous but causally coordinated processes: continuous audio encoding, LLM token generation, and TTS synthesis from the speaking hidden states. Complete token sequence example. We present unit-by-unit token trace for single-turn interaction. The user asks via speech for summary of One Hundred Years of Solitude by Gabriel Garcıa Marquez. The trace uses the abbreviated notation from 3.2. <im start>system You are Listen-Write-Speak model with three channels: listening, visible writing, and speaking. <im end> <im start>user <audio start> a1 aN <audio end><im end> Listening phase (Units 16). Each Listening Unit ingests audio tokens and generates ls cogn output. The per-Unit structure is: <unit> a1 a10 <lc> content </lc> </unit>. Units 12 produce empty visible-writing output while audio is still arriving; from Unit 3 onward, the model incrementally interprets the users intent: Unit 1: (empty) Unit 2: (empty) Unit 3: The user is asking for short piece on Gabriel Garcıa Marquezs novel. Unit 4: The novels full title is being revealed: One Hundred Years of Solitude. The Unit 5: full title is now complete, confirming the subject is the novel by Garcıa Unit 6: Marquez. The user wants concise, insightful summary of the novels themes and style. Speaking phase (Units 719). Upon detecting end-of-turn, the model transitions to Speaking Units. Each Unit simultaneously generates speak tokens (streamed to TTS for real-time voice) and reply cogn tokens (displayed as on-screen text). The parallel channel outputs are: Unit 7: speak: Sure! *One Hundred; reply cogn: *One Hundred Years of Solitude* Unit 8: speak: Years of Solitude* is; reply cogn: by Gabriel Garcıa Marquez is Unit 9: speak: magical real; reply cogn: landmark of magical realism, chronicling Unit 10: speak: ist masterpiece about the; reply cogn: seven generations of the Buendıa family Unit 11: speak: Buendıa family; reply cogn: in the mythical town of Macondo. Unit 12: speak: in Macondo,; reply cogn: Blending myth, history, and fantasy, Unit 13: speak: blending myth and; reply cogn: the novel explores themes of time, Unit 14: speak: reality to explore; reply cogn: fate, and isolation, weaving Unit 15: speak: time,; reply cogn: dreamlike narrative where the extraordinary feels Unit 16: speak: fate, and isolation; reply cogn: ordinary. With lyrical prose and rich Unit 17: speak: through a; reply cogn: symbolism, Garcıa Marquez creates an Unit 18: speak: dreamlike, ly; reply cogn: enduring tale of love, madness, and Unit 19: speak: rical story. <turn eos>; reply cogn: the inescapable cycles of human experience. Decoded outputs. Concatenating each channels per-Unit tokens yields the final userfacing content: Speaking (speak): Sure! One Hundred Years of Solitude is magical realist masterpiece about the Buendıa family in Macondo, blending myth and reality to explore time, fate, and isolation through dreamlike, lyrical story. Visible Writing (reply cogn): One Hundred Years of Solitude by Gabriel Garcıa Marquez is landmark of magical realism, chronicling seven generations of the Buendıa family in the mythical town of Macondo. Blending myth, history, and fantasy, the novel explores themes of time, fate, and isolation, weaving dreamlike narrative where the extraordinary feels ordinary. With lyrical prose and rich symbolism, Garcıa Marquez creates an enduring tale of love, madness, and the inescapable cycles of human experience. The asymmetry is evident: speak produces one-sentence response suitable for spoken delivery, while reply cogn simultaneously generates detailed multi-sentence literary analysis displayed as on-screen text. This text-only trace makes explicit how the token schema realizes the core principle that visible writing is the primary, unconstrained output modality.

sections.title: B Data Construction Details
sections.content: This appendix provides the full details of the two-stage data construction pipeline summarized in 4. At high level, Stage 1 uses teacher LLM to synthesize per-second cognitive annotations from text QA pairs, and Stage 2 aligns these annotations with real audio via CTC timestamps to construct the final Unit-based token sequences, with optional interruption augmentation. B.1 Stage 1: Offline cognitive synthesis Given text conversation pair, we use strong text LLM to generate per-second cognitive annotations intended to be causally consistent with the revealed input timeline. 13 Speech rate estimation. We first estimate the number of seconds the spoken response will occupy, using empirical speech rates: 7 characters/second for Chinese and 3.5 words/second for English. The response is segmented into per-second chunks accordingly. Causal constraint prompting. We prompt the LLM to simulate streaming reasoning process: at each second t, the model generates writing text based only on what has been heard up to second (for listening-phase writing, ls cogn) or what has been said up to second (for reply-phase writing, reply cogn). This prompt enforces causal constraint that prevents the model from using future information. Quality control. We use structured output (Pydantic schema) to guarantee format validity, and filter samples where per-second writing text exceeds character budget or the voice response is empty. B.2 Stage 2: Online timeline construction Stage 2 takes real audio recordings and CTC-based character-level alignments, together with the Stage 1 text outputs, and constructs the final token sequences. Global timeline construction. We build second-by-second timeline for the entire multiturn conversation, inserting random silence intervals between turns to simulate natural conversational rhythm. Unit type allocation. Each Unit is assigned type based on who is speaking and whether speech text is available: If the AI is speaking and has speak text Speaking Unit (with reply cogn) Otherwise Listening Unit (with ls cogn) Listening Units are further categorized as early (user not yet speaking), effective (user speaking), and post (after user finishes, before AI speaks), each with appropriate writing content. Why separate the listening and speaking phases? We provide the formal justification for using separate tags for listening-phase writing (ls cogn) and reply-phase writing (reply cogn), rather than single unified <cogn> tag. Different conditional distributions. The two writing modes are conditioned on fundamentally different information: ls cogn (listening-phase writing): Plc(yt aT, h), conditioned only on audio history and prior writing. Its role is incremental understanding of the users intent. reply cogn (reply-phase writing): Prc(yt aT, sT, h), additionally conditioned on the models own speech output. Its role is producing structured output (code, analysis, Markdown) that goes beyond what is being said aloud. Explicit state reduces conditional entropy. Let st = {lc, rc, spk} denote the semantic state of token yt, corresponding to listening-phase writing, reply-phase writing, and speaking respectively. With unified tag, the model must implicitly infer the state, facing mixture distribution: Pmixed(yt) = sS πs Ps(yt) where πs = P(s y<t, x). By Jensens inequality applied to entropys concavity: H(Pmixed) πs H(Ps) 14 (3) (4) Since the three state-conditional distributions differ substantially (ls cogn produces incremental understanding, reply cogn produces code or structured analysis, and speak produces natural spoken language), the KL divergences DKL(PsPs ) are large, and the inequality is strict. Explicit state markers eliminate this mixture penalty, reducing the per-token prediction entropy. Causal consistency in full-duplex. Full-duplex interaction imposes strict temporal causal constraint: the models speech at time may only depend on user audio up to time and prior writing, never on future input. The token schema encodes this causal chain explicitly: ls cogn reply cogn speak (5) The </lc> token marks the boundary where listening-phase writing ends and reply-phase writing begins, ensuring that speaking generation cannot leak information from listeningphase writing segment that has not yet concluded. With unified <cogn> tag, the boundary between still understanding the user and already producing the reply is ambiguous, creating risk of causal violation during training.

sections.title: D Data Construction Prompt Template
sections.content: We provide the complete prompt template used in Stage 1 (B.1) to synthesize per-second causal cognitive annotations from text QA pairs using Qwen3-235B. The prompt enforces strict causal constraint: at each second t, the model may only reason about input received up to second t. Input preparation. Given user instruction, we first segment it into per-second chunks using speech-rate estimation. Let denote the total number of seconds. Substantive reasoning begins at second t0 = min(2, T); earlier seconds serve as ASR-style confirmation. System Prompt You are an AI assistant that emulates streaming reasoning. ## Context User audio arrives second by second. At second you only know text from second 0 to N. ## Output Structure 1. `streaming reasoning chain` ({T - t0} entries): - `second`: {t0} {T - 1} - `reasoning this second`: based only on known input, {C-10}{C+10} characters long 2. `voice response`: concise, friendly spoken reply based on the complete text response, length should be around 30%40% of the original response. Avoid introducing details that are not in the original response. ## Instructions - Keep per-second reasoning length stable; even early seconds should stay in range. - Early seconds may be ASR-style confirmations ("I heard...") while waiting for more input. - Never mention content that arrives after the current second. - streaming reasoning chain should start producing substantive reasoning at second {t0}. - The final voice response must reflect the full written reply. where is the total number of user-input seconds, t0 is the reasoning start second, and is the target characters per second. 15 User Prompt ### User input by second: Second 0: "{segment 0}" Second 1: "{segment 1}" ... Second {T-1}: "{segment {T-1}}" ### Written reply: {original response} Follow the instructions and emit `StreamingColloquialRewrite`. Streaming reasoning starts at second {t0}. The model is required to return structured JSON object (enforced via Pydantic schema StreamingColloquialRewrite) containing: (1) streaming reasoning chain, list of persecond reasoning entries, and (2) voice response, natural spoken paraphrase. We use temperature 0.3 and structured output parsing to guarantee format validity. An equivalent Chinese-language prompt is used for Chinese data, with identical constraints and structure.

sections.title: E Evaluation Prompts for Reply Quality
sections.content: We provide the three judge prompts used for the reply-quality evaluation in 5.2.2, which is conducted via GPT-5. All prompts operate on the text transcription of the user instruction and the model response. The placeholders {question} and {prediction} are filled with the user instruction transcription and the model response transcription, respectively. Evaluation Prompt: Overall Quality (Mean Score) need your help to evaluate the performance of several models in the speech interaction scenario. The models will receive speech input from the user, which they need to understand and respond to with speech output. Your task is to rate the models responses based on the provided user input transcription [Instruction] and the models output transcription [Response]. Please evaluate the response on scale of 1 to 5: 1 point: Largely irrelevant, incorrect, or off-topic. 2 points: Somewhat relevant but lacks accuracy/completeness. 3 points: Relevant and mostly accurate, may lack conciseness. 4 points: Relevant, accurate, concise, clear answer. 5 points: Exceptionally relevant, accurate, and to the point. ### [Instruction]: {question} ### [Response]: {prediction} After evaluating, please output the score only without anything else. Evaluation Prompt: Naturalness Help evaluate the naturalness of the model response in speech interaction scenario. Naturalness: How much does the reply sound like natural, fluent, everyday spoken conversation (not formal written language or robotic)? Rate the reply on scale from 1 to 5: 1 -- Very unnatural, robotic, completely lacks spoken quality. 2 -- Mostly unnatural, noticeably different from real speech. 3 -- Generally natural, minor awkwardness in phrasing. 4 -- Natural and conversational, resembling authentic speech. 5 -- Extremely natural, indistinguishable from spontaneous talk. ### [Instruction]: {question} ### [Response]: {prediction} Output: Naturalness Score: Naturalness Explanation: ... 16 Evaluation Prompt: Factual Accuracy Help evaluate the factual accuracy of the model response in speech interaction scenario. Factual Accuracy: Is all information mentioned factual and correct, with no hallucinations or made-up/incorrect content? Rate the reply on scale from 1 to 5: 1 -- Clearly incorrect, contains hallucinations or false info. 2 -- Partially correct, notable errors or hallucinations. 3 -- Mostly correct and factual, minor inaccuracies. 4 -- Accurate, no hallucinations, maybe minor details to refine. 5 -- Fully factual, every detail accurate, no hallucination. ### [Instruction]: {question} ### [Response]: {prediction} Output: Accuracy Score: Accuracy Explanation: ...
